cases.input: Held: Courts should review the Board’s determination that a given set of facts does not rise to the level of “persecution” under a substantial-evidence standard. Pp. 3–10.
wantSentences: Held: Courts should review the Board’s determination that a given set of facts does not rise to the level of “persecution” under a substantial-evidence standard. Pp. 3–10.

cases.input: The Court has previously interpreted this provision to prescribe a deferential, “substantial-evidence standard,” Nasrallah v. Barr, 590 U. S. 573, 584, under which administrative findings of fact are “conclusive unless any reasonable adjudicator would be compelled to conclude to the contrary,” 8 U. S. C. §1252(b)(4)(B).
wantSentences: The Court has previously interpreted this provision to prescribe a deferential, “substantial-evidence standard,” Nasrallah v. Barr, 590 U. S. 573, 584, under which administrative findings of fact are “conclusive unless any reasonable adjudicator would be compelled to conclude to the contrary,” 8 U. S. C. §1252(b)(4)(B).

cases.input: The court rejected that argument. Id., at 484. The Government presses a different theory.
wantSentences: The Government presses a different theory.

cases.input: Under the INA, the U. S. Government “may grant asylum” to a noncitizen if it “determines” that he “is a refugee.” 8 U. S. C. §1158(b)(1)(A).
wantSentences: Under the INA, the U. S. Government “may grant asylum” to a noncitizen if it “determines” that he “is a refugee.” 8 U. S. C. §1158(b)(1)(A).

cases.input: Substantial evidence means only “such relevant evidence as a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a conclusion.” Biestek v. Berryhill, 587 U. S. 97, 103.
wantSentences: Substantial evidence means only “such relevant evidence as a reasonable mind might accept as adequate to support a conclusion.” Biestek v. Berryhill, 587 U. S. 97, 103.
